item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of a satirical science article I read in the Annals of Improbable Research, treating 18-wheeler trucks as some weird family of insects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why I dont use public transport

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always love big crawlers like this. +1

Minor nitpick on style. I think few paragraphs should be merged together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this and the original article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had exactly the opposite problem. These things blend right into modern society, grow incredibly quickly, and reproduce in large liters. They have no natural predators. They're top predators that appear to grow ten pounds of bus steak for every pound of human steak consumed.

My question is not "how do they survive," it's "why hasn't humanity been overrun by these things long ago?"

My personal headcannon is it's a MCD deal that went south. Purchase a bus from one universe over where they run on homeless people instead of gasoline, use to murder some guy as a prank… oh, it escaped. How droll. Let's sip cocktails and place bets on how many generations of busses manage to breed in the wild before those bumbling buffoons at the Foundation manage to destroy… excuse me, capture and breed them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Because of their short lifespans and high reproduction rates, the number of SCP-2086 instances in Foundation custody shifts regularly." Maybe I'm nitpicking, but I would like to see how the foundation keeps these organisms alive in its custody. Also, where do they keep coming from in nature if the foundation keeps destroying colonies as they're found? I'm at a neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short and creepy. I like it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of an animal that is able to look and function indistinguishably from an automobile is cool, although as others having pointed out, it's been done before: SCP-1524. Please try to research the other entries and make use of the search bar.

Also, how do their "wheels" work if they are actually legs? Can they actually drive on them? Rotating body parts don't occur in nature, and so that's something you always want to address.

I do like it enough to give it a +1 though. The thought that cities with their vermin problems are home to bugs this big and this sinister is a neat trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plain, no actual story to this, and just one more spooky-scary without anything to distinguish it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So you did post it after all? Nice. +1; I enjoyed the cleverness of the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like how this is written, it feels more like a list of elements to go into a polished, well-written article than an actual SCP. The frequent line breaks, and the brief descriptions of portions of the effect, bored me, and none of this covered territory that hasn't been done before. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to make this pun, then realized that they are buses, not VWs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to be first to make this pun D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why so many paragraph breaks?

item.title: Staff Post- Closed
item.content:encoded: I N F A M Y, you've been leaving a very large number of useless, contentless, and/or incorrect posts on the forums. Consider this an official warning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hits all the right marks for me - bizarre, well written, and unique. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really do like this.
Bug buses get an upvote from me.

item.wikidot:authorName: I N F A M Y

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I have to.
- Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice and weird. Don't think it should be Keter but I like the straightforwardness of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possible balancing of the an ecosystem?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly. I was thinking something more along the lines of there being an even more dangerous anomaly that disguises itself as public transit users. These bugs are killing them. And yes, Fae, welcome to the bug bus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any room for a third?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's do it!

An early investigation into the phenomenon, with first theories that people are being shanghaied into alternate dimensions? Discovering that no, it's just bugs trying to eat, would make it feel comfortably mundane, no?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a public transit user, this sufficiently creeps me out. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the imagery of a bus with beetle-type wings and creepy insect legs flying around, but it's not enough to carry what is, essentially, an article that's been done countless times before. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's really nothing here. They're just giant bugs that look like buses. No story or anything interesting. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not the most amazing article (I kind of kept wanting it to go one step further toward the end), but it works and described well. Anomalous arthropods never get old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.badumtss.net

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… these are Volkswagon Beetles, then? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tend to prefer longer articles but this is well executed and an interesting concept, especially the use of the dead. I'm reminded somewhat of assassin bugs covering themselves in the corpses of ants while they infiltrate a colony…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no-voting, but leaning towards down, purely for clarity issues. See below:

+ show block
– hide block

Side points first:

Accidental civilian observation of SCP-2086 instances engaging in this activity is minimized due to the significantly smaller size of juvenile specimens.

I don't buy this. I mean, we're still talking about bus route signs in major towns and cities being stolen by cars using wheels as limbs. I'd find it more credible if, perhaps, you specified that juveniles are nocturnal? I don't live in a city, though, so take it with a grain of salt.

Also, style wise, I find the paragraph describing the human corpse decoy and the final paragraph about how it is made either unnecessarily repetitive or oddly split up. It'd be nice if there was a way to shift those together.

On to the big clarity issues:

A newborn specimen of SCP-2086 can be expected to grow to maturity within one week.

Mature instances of SCP-2086 do not feed, instead living off of nutrients consumed as juveniles.

Each passenger is then forcefully removed from the mature instance by a juvenile…. (Feeding and preservation process)

1: Is that a week to reach juvenile size or full maturity? If juvenile, how long does it take a juvenile instance to become a mature one? What is the typical lifespan of a 2086?
2: Does a juvenile only ever need to eat one corpse? If not, how many more corpses does it need before becoming mature, and what does it do with them?

Basically, if your intention that these things die off like mayflies, breed like crazy, and only need one human corpse per instance, I dig it. That gives it a frenetic intensity and a potential to snowball that earns the Keter.

As written, you're just a few hard data points short of getting that across. Specify a lifespan and the one corpse thing (and maybe some birth rates or gestation times if you want to get really fancy), and I would love this to pieces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You and I need to write a tale featuring this SCP wherein this line makes sense. You down?

item.title: This is truly a work of art!
item.wikidot:authorName: I N F A M Y
item.content:encoded: This is a master piece! It has the perfect amount of fine tuned humor but not to much to throw the creep factor out of whack. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We can rest easy, knowing that there are giant bugs out there, eating users of public transit."

I have heard a few times that the majority of written sentences are novel, and have not been written before. I tend not to believe it until I read sentences like this.

Also, have a +1. This is a great idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would say that their origin would be of concern, especially since they are classified Keter. A majority of these things are not contained at all (according to your article), and their high reproduction rate would be such a hassle that it is fair to say that the Foundation would want a more permanent way of containing these creatures.

To that end, it is fair that the in-universe Foundation would attempt finding out their origins in effort to contain them for good.

My main reason for upvote is the implications behind the SCP themselves, rather than anything from the SCP document itself.

EDIT: Colab? But that may or may ruin any other headcanons? What is your headcanon, Author-san?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I suppose the Foundation doesn't really have much investment into where they came from, when documenting and containing them is a higher priority. I'm sure that, in-universe, they have files and theories on their origins. Personally, when I was writing this, I thought of them as a species that evolved at an anomalously fast rate, even gaining minor sapience in the process.

And yeah, they just lay eggs.

Also, thank you all for the upvotes! It means a lot to me!

EDIT: I like your headcanon better than mine, mind if I steal it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will admit I am struggling between upvoting and downvoting the article. For the moment, I will settle for neutral vote… until I come to a verdict.

Pros:

  • The article is fairly straightforward and direct in the delivery of the topic.
  • It is somewhat unhinging for the thing to use a corpse in that manner.

Cons:

  • While you have clearly explained the creature's features and habits, I feel like there's more we could potentially know. Why are they shaped like buses? Did they evolve to hunt humans in their "natural habitat"? If so, evolution normally takes thousands and millions of years to achieve? (Maybe someone could write a Tale about their origins perhaps)

P.S. How do they reproduce? I'm guessing by laying eggs though…

EDIT: Oh goddamn… Upvote it is! Headcanon below:

+ show block
– hide block

And I got this headcanon that this SCP are creatures from a far-flung future where Earth becomes a Coruscant-type planet with humanity numbering in the trillions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp, it would seem the 2086 curse is lifted. We can rest easy, knowing that there are giant bugs out there, eating users of public transit.

A very creeped-out +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are at least three or four other skips this reminds me of, but the thought of flying buses tickled me enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite simple, but it's a neat and creepy idea, executed really damn well, and it's just overall well-done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels like familiar territory, but the use of corpses as lures tickled me. Have +1.
